data.title: Is Flowserve (FLS) Still Attractive After Its Recent 18.5% One Month Share Price Decline
data.description:
• If you are wondering whether Flowserve's current share price still offers value, it helps to look past the headline moves and focus on what the underlying numbers are saying about the stock.
  • The share price recently closed at US$72.15, with returns of 1.1% over 7 days, a 18.5% decline over 30 days, 2.0% year to date, 50.0% over 1 year, 123.1% over 3 years, and 102.4% over 5 years. This gives a mixed picture of shorter term swings versus longer term performance.
  • Recent coverage around Flowserve has focused on its role in the broader capital goods space and how investor sentiment is adjusting following these share price moves. This context matters because it can influence how much weight the market is putting on future projects, balance sheet strength, and cash generation rather than just the latest headlines.
  • Flowserve currently has a valuation score of 4/6, based on being assessed as undervalued on four out of six checks. The sections that follow will compare different valuation approaches before finishing with a framework that can help you judge whether those methods are giving you the full picture.

Flowserve delivered 50.0% returns over the last year. See how this stacks up to the rest of the Machinery industry.

Approach 1: Flowserve Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) Analysis

A Discounted Cash Flow, or DCF, model estimates what a company might be worth by projecting its future cash flows and then discounting those back to today to get a present value per share.

For Flowserve, the model used is a 2 Stage Free Cash Flow to Equity approach, built on cash flow projections in $. The latest twelve month free cash flow is about $429.7 million. Analyst inputs and extrapolated estimates in this model have free cash flow reaching about $805.6 million in ten years, with the near term years anchored by forecasts and the later years extended by Simply Wall St to smooth the path.

When all those projected cash flows are discounted back, the DCF model arrives at an estimated intrinsic value of about $87.60 per share. Relative to the recent share price of $72.15, this model output implies Flowserve trades at roughly a 17.6% discount to that intrinsic value.

Result: UNDERVALUED (according to this DCF model)

Approach 2: Flowserve Price vs Earnings

For profitable companies, the P/E ratio is a useful way to gauge what you are paying for each dollar of current earnings. It links the share price directly to earnings, which many investors watch closely when deciding how much they are prepared to pay for a stock.

What counts as a normal or fair P/E often reflects the balance between growth expectations and risk. Higher expected earnings growth or lower perceived risk can support a higher P/E, while slower growth or higher risk usually justifies a lower one.

Flowserve currently trades on a P/E of 26.52x. This sits slightly above the Machinery industry average of 26.08x and below the peer group average of 30.74x. Simply Wall St also calculates a Fair Ratio of 37.05x for Flowserve. This Fair Ratio is a proprietary estimate of the P/E that could be justified by factors such as earnings growth profile, industry, profit margins, market cap and specific risks.

Because it incorporates these company specific drivers, the Fair Ratio can be more informative than a simple comparison with industry or peer averages. With Flowserve's current P/E of 26.52x below the Fair Ratio of 37.05x, this framework suggests the shares trade at a discount to that implied level.

Approach 1: BlackRock Excess Returns Analysis

The Excess Returns model evaluates how much profit a company is expected to generate above the return that shareholders require, given its equity risk. Instead of focusing on raw earnings alone, it compares those earnings to the value of the equity invested in the business.

For BlackRock, the model uses a Book Value of US$360.41 per share and a Stable EPS estimate of US$57.62 per share, sourced from weighted future Return on Equity estimates from 6 analysts. The Average Return on Equity is 16.17%, while the Cost of Equity is US$28.20 per share. This results in an Excess Return of US$29.41 per share, reflecting the difference between what the equity is expected to earn and what shareholders are assumed to require.

The Stable Book Value input is US$356.34 per share, based on weighted future Book Value estimates from 4 analysts. Combining these assumptions, the Excess Returns model produces an estimated intrinsic value of about US$1,009 per share, which implies the shares trade at roughly a 7.5% discount to this estimate.
